emails.subject: CDR framework update - post-2018 review
emails.body:
R,

Following last quarter's model validation cycle I wanted to document some changes to how I am thinking about CDR calibration going forward.

The 2018 vintage data from CoreLogic is now showing early payment stress in a few high-LTV buckets we had previously treated as benign. Nothing alarming, but enough to make me want to tighten the base case assumptions for pools with weighted average LTV above 82%.

My current thinking:
- Pools with WA LTV below 78% and WA FICO above 720: base CDR in the 1.8-2.2% range remains defensible
- Pools with WA LTV 78-85% or WA FICO 680-720: I am moving the floor to 2.5%, with upward adjustments for geographic concentration
- Pools above 85% LTV or below 680 FICO: minimum 3.5%, stress case 5.5%

I will bake this into the assumption tab for the next deal. Worth aligning with T. Roadrunner before the next credit committee.

Wile

emails.subject: RE: Mesa Verde Trust 2019-1 - assumption review
emails.body:
T,

Responding to your questions on the Mesa Verde assumptions.

On CDR: I landed at 2.4% base case. The pool WA LTV is 79.3% and WA FICO is 711, which puts it squarely in the middle bucket I described to R. Runner back in March. The geographic split is reasonably diversified - Texas at 18%, Florida 14%, California 22%, rest spread across 12 states. HHI comes out at 0.14 which is acceptable. No single state concentration trigger.

On CPR: 12% base case, 18% stress. The pool has average seasoning of 8 months which is relatively fresh. I am not giving credit for seasoning here.

Loss severity at 38% blended. Judicial states represent about 31% of the pool by balance - Florida being the biggest contributor. Non-judicial at 28%, judicial at 52% for those exposures.

Let me know if you want to walk through the waterfall sensitivity before Friday.

Wile

emails.subject: RE: CDR stress multiples - 2008 calibration question
emails.body:
Wile,

Quick question ahead of the validation committee Thursday. In the model documentation you reference stress multiples calibrated to 2001 and 2008 downturns. For the 2008 scenario, what multiple are you applying to base CDR and what is the assumed loss severity floor?

I want to make sure I can defend this to the rating agencies if they push on it.

T.

emails.subject: RE: CDR stress multiples - 2008 calibration question
emails.body:
T,

For the 2008 stress scenario I apply a 2.8x multiple to base CDR with a floor of 4.5% regardless of pool quality. Loss severity floor in that scenario is 48% blended, rising to 62% for judicial states.

The rationale: peak observed CDR in the 2008-2010 window for comparable non-agency pools was in the 7-9% range on base cases that were set around 2.5-3.5%. That is roughly a 2.5-3.0x multiple. I use 2.8x as a conservative midpoint.

For 2001 I use a 1.6x multiple with a floor of 3.0%. That cycle was meaningfully less severe for residential mortgage credit.

This has held up in two prior validation cycles. Moody's did not push back in the Mesa Verde review.

Wile

emails.subject: Redrock Mortgage Trust 2020-1 - pre-deal assumption memo
emails.body:
R,

Attaching the assumption memo for the Redrock deal ahead of Thursday credit committee.

Key numbers:
- CDR base: 2.7% - pool WA LTV is 81.1%, WA FICO 703. Geographic concentration is elevated - Arizona at 26% and Nevada at 19%. Combined that is 45% in two states that had severe HPA declines in 2008. I am adding 30bps to the base CDR to reflect that concentration risk.
- CPR: 14% base. Pool is 11 months seasoned on average. Starting to give a little seasoning credit here but not much.
- Loss severity: 41% blended. Higher than Mesa Verde because of the Arizona/Nevada weight - both are non-judicial but had long resolution timelines in the last cycle.

Equity IRR at base case is 11.2%. At 2.8x CDR stress it drops to 6.8%. I am comfortable with that range.

Wile

emails.subject: COVID adjustment to CDR framework - interim guidance
emails.body:
T,

Given the forbearance volumes we are seeing I want to document a temporary overlay to the CDR framework.

Forbearance rates in the broader non-agency universe are running 8-11% of balances. Historically forbearance converts to permanent default at roughly 15-25% depending on the vintage and borrower profile. That is a meaningful tail risk for any pool we are structuring right now.

For deals closing between now and Q1 2021 I am adding a COVID overlay of 40-60bps to base CDR depending on the share of the pool in states with extended foreclosure moratoria. States with active moratoria get the higher end of that range because the timing of loss crystallization is less predictable.

This is a temporary overlay. I will revisit once we have 12 months of post-forbearance performance data.

Wile

emails.subject: RE: Sunrise Mortgage Trust 2021-3 - early pool tape
emails.body:
Wile,

The originator just sent over the preliminary pool tape for Sunrise 2021-3. I know it is early but can you take a first look and flag anything that stands out? They want to close in Q1 2021 and I want us to be prepared.

Pool is roughly 1,200 loans, ~$380M. Originator says WA LTV around 80%, WA FICO around 715. Geographic mix is apparently heavy Southeast.

R.

emails.subject: RE: Sunrise Mortgage Trust 2021-3 - early pool tape
emails.body:
R,

Took a first pass at the tape. A few observations.

The Southeast concentration is real - Florida 28%, Georgia 17%, South Carolina 11%. That is 56% in three states. Florida alone triggers my single-state concentration watch at 25%+. HHI is coming out around 0.19 which is on the high side.

WA LTV confirmed at 80.2%, WA FICO at 716. Those are fine on their own. But the geographic concentration combined with the Florida judicial foreclosure exposure gives me pause. Florida had some of the longest resolution timelines in the country during the last cycle.

I also notice average seasoning is only 4 months. Very fresh pool.

I will wait for the final tape before setting assumptions but wanted you to have the directional read.

Wile

emails.subject: Sunrise 2021-3 - updated pool analysis
emails.body:
R, T,

Final tape is in. Pool settled at $392M, 1,247 loans. Key stats:

WA LTV: 80.4%
WA FICO: 714
WA seasoning: 5 months
Geographic: FL 27%, GA 16%, SC 12%, NC 9%, other 36%
HHI: 0.188
Judicial state exposure: 34% by balance (Florida)

I have been working through the CDR calibration. Starting point from my framework is 2.5% for a pool with these LTV/FICO characteristics. From there:

+25bps for geographic concentration (HHI above 0.17, single state above 25%)
+30bps for COVID overlay (Florida is under an extended foreclosure moratorium, falls in the higher overlay bucket from my August note)
+15bps for seasoning adjustment (5 months is very fresh, I do not give seasoning credit below 12 months and apply a small penalty below 6)

That gets me to 3.2% before any additional stress. I think that is the right base case for this pool.

Will confirm final numbers after I run the waterfall sensitivity. Credit committee memo to follow.

Wile

emails.subject: RE: Sunrise 2021-3 - CDR question
emails.body:
Wile,

One follow-up on the Sunrise CDR. The 3.2% base is higher than what we used on Redrock (2.7%) even though the LTV and FICO profiles are similar. Can you help me document the delta for the validation file?

T.

emails.subject: RE: Sunrise 2021-3 - CDR question
emails.body:
T,

Good question, happy to document this.

Redrock vs Sunrise CDR delta:

Redrock base was 2.4% from the LTV/FICO grid, with +30bps for geographic concentration (AZ/NV). No COVID overlay because that deal closed pre-COVID. Total: 2.7%.

Sunrise base is also 2.5% from the grid (slightly higher because LTV is 80.4% vs Redrock's 81.1% - actually Redrock should have been the same, but I had refined the grid by the time Sunrise came around). Then:
- +25bps geographic concentration (FL/GA/SC, HHI 0.188)
- +30bps COVID overlay (FL moratorium, higher overlay bucket)
- +15bps seasoning penalty (5 months)

Total: 3.2%

The COVID overlay and the seasoning penalty are the two components that did not exist for Redrock. Happy to provide this write-up for the validation file.

Wile

emails.subject: Sunrise 2021-3 - credit committee memo
emails.body:
R, T,

Attaching the credit committee memo for Sunrise 2021-3. Deal is on track to close late February.

Highlights:
- CDR 3.2% base, 8.96% stress (2.8x)
- CPR 11% base (no seasoning credit, fresh pool)
- Loss severity 40% blended (34% judicial weight, FL extended timelines)
- OC trigger: Class A at 115%, Class B at 107%
- Equity IRR at base: 10.4%. At stress: 5.9%

Senior tranche sized at AAA with 32% CE. Moody's MILAN output was consistent with prior deals.

One note for the file: the 3.2% CDR reflects standard framework plus three overlays specific to this pool. Documentation is in the assumption tab and T. Roadrunner has the written reconciliation.

Wile

emails.subject: RE: post-COVID CDR overlay - revisit timing
emails.body:
R,

You asked last week when I plan to revisit the COVID overlay. Here is my thinking.

Forbearance exit data is now coming in and the permanent default conversion rate is tracking at the lower end of my 15-25% range. That is encouraging. However, foreclosure moratoria are still in place in several states including Florida, which means we have not seen the full resolution picture yet.

My plan: keep the overlay in place through Q3 2021 for any deal with meaningful Florida exposure. Revisit after we see two quarters of post-moratorium performance data. I will not remove the overlay for Sunrise 2021-3 retrospectively - that assumption is set and the deal is closed.

For new deals starting now I am reducing the overlay by 10bps. Full removal likely Q4 2021 at the earliest.

Wile

emails.subject: Sunrise 2021-3 - 6 month performance check
emails.body:
T,

Quick performance update on Sunrise 2021-3 now that we have 6 months of remittance data.

Actual 60+ day delinquency is running at 1.1% of current balance. Annualized that implies a CDR somewhere in the 1.4-1.8% range, well below our 3.2% base assumption. OC tests are passing comfortably on both Class A and B.

Not surprising - I would expect a fresh pool to look clean in the first 6 months. The real test will be 18-24 months when the COVID-era forbearances have fully resolved and the seasoning curve kicks in.

I still think 3.2% was the right base case given what we knew at close. Conservative but justified.

Wile

emails.subject: Blue Canyon Trust 2022-1 - assumption summary
emails.body:
R, T,

Assumptions for Blue Canyon 2022-1:

This pool is cleaner than Sunrise. WA LTV 77.8%, WA FICO 728, average seasoning 14 months. Geographic mix is much more diversified - no state above 16%, HHI at 0.09.

CDR: 2.0% base. Starting from the 2.5% grid floor, then:
- Minus 25bps for FICO/LTV quality (both better than grid midpoint)
- Minus 30bps for geographic diversification (HHI well below 0.15)
- Minus 15bps for seasoning credit (14 months, giving partial credit)
- No COVID overlay (removing it for new deals given current data)

Total: 2.1%, rounded to 2.0% for conservatism.

This is the cleanest pool I have seen in three years. Equity IRR at base is 13.1%.

Wile

emails.subject: Rising rate environment - CPR sensitivity note
emails.body:
R,

With the Fed starting its hiking cycle I want to flag an important sensitivity for current and pipeline deals.

Rising rates suppress prepayments. For pools structured with a 12-15% CPR assumption, if actual CPR drops to 6-8% (which is plausible in a 400bps rate hike environment), WAL extends materially. A 30-year pool structured at 12% CPR has a WAL of roughly 4.8 years. At 6% CPR that extends to 7.2 years.

For equity holders this is mostly a timing issue - cash flows arrive later but the credit losses are what drive IRR, not WAL. For senior note holders it affects price.

I am not changing base CPR assumptions yet but I am adding a low-CPR sensitivity scenario (6%) to the equity return tab for all new deals. Worth flagging to investors.

Wile

emails.subject: Annual model validation - VBA documentation gap
emails.body:
Wile,

As part of this year's SR 11-7 validation cycle I need to flag a finding.

The cash flow engine VBA in the CLO model has not been formally documented since version 2.1. We are now on version 3.2. The validation committee is asking for a plain-English description of the waterfall logic, the OC/IC trigger mechanics, and the basis for any hardcoded constants in the macros.

I know the model works - back-testing results have been consistently conservative. But I cannot sign off on the validation without documentation of the logic. Can we schedule time to go through it?

T.

emails.subject: RE: Annual model validation - VBA documentation gap
emails.body:
T,

Acknowledged. I know this has been an open item for a while.

I built the waterfall engine in stages between 2017 and 2019 and did not document it as I went. The core logic is sound - I can walk you through it verbally - but writing it up properly will take a few days of focused work.

Let's schedule a session for mid-July. I will come prepared with a module-by-module walkthrough. I can document it into the model risk file as we go.

In the meantime I can tell you that the hardcoded constants in the OC/IC trigger logic are derived from the rating agency criteria in effect when the model was last updated (2019 Moody's MILAN framework). They have not been touched since.

Wile

emails.subject: Sunrise 2021-3 - 18 month update
emails.body:
R,

18-month remittance update on Sunrise 2021-3.

Actual 60+ delinquency is at 2.3% of current balance. Implied CDR is running approximately 2.6-2.8% annualized. Still tracking below our 3.2% base assumption but closing the gap as the pool seasons and some of the Florida forbearance overhang resolves.

OC tests continue to pass. No cash flow diversion events.

This is broadly what I expected. The 3.2% base was set to account for exactly this kind of seasoning curve and post-forbearance resolution. We are on track.

Wile

emails.subject: Desert Spring Trust 2023-1 - assumption memo
emails.body:
R, T,

Assumptions for Desert Spring 2023-1. This is an interesting pool.

WA LTV: 78.9%, WA FICO: 722. Solid fundamentals. But geographic concentration is back - Texas at 31%, Arizona at 22%. HHI at 0.21, highest I have seen in a while.

CDR: 2.8% base.
- Grid starting point: 2.3% (LTV/FICO quality adjustment, minus 20bps from floor)
- +35bps geographic concentration (HHI above 0.20 triggers the higher concentration penalty in my framework)
- +15bps for Texas - while non-judicial, Texas had an unusual pattern of prolonged default-to-foreclosure timelines in stressed periods. I apply a state-specific overlay for TX concentration above 25%.
- No COVID overlay (fully retired as of Q1 2023)
- Minus 10bps partial seasoning credit (9 months average)

This is a conservative number for a pool with good credit characteristics. The concentration is the driver.

Wile

emails.subject: RE: VBA documentation - status
emails.body:
T,

I know we still have the VBA documentation as an open item from last year's validation. I have not forgotten.

I want to be direct with you: the waterfall engine has about 1,400 lines of VBA and the logic is not linear. It was written incrementally and some of the subroutines call each other in ways that made sense when I was building it but will be hard to document without essentially rewriting the explanation from scratch.

I am planning to do a full documentation pass this summer before the next validation cycle. What I would suggest in the interim is that nobody modifies the macro code without coming to me first. There are dependencies that are not obvious from reading the code.

I take full responsibility for this gap. It is on my list.

Wile

emails.subject: Thoughts on current vintage risk
emails.body:
R,

Sharing some observations on the current environment as we think about pipeline deals.

The combination of elevated home prices (WA LTV in new originations is deceptively low because of HPA) and higher rates creating payment shock risk in ARM pools is something I am watching closely. For fixed-rate pools the payment risk is lower but the prepayment suppression from rates means WAL extension is real.

For CDR calibration I am thinking about this differently than prior vintages. The LTV/FICO-based grid was calibrated to historical default experience where the starting point was a more normalized rate environment. Borrowers who originated at 7%+ rates have less refinancing optionality, which changes the prepayment/default tradeoff.

My working hypothesis: for 2023 vintage pools, CDR risk is actually slightly lower than LTV/FICO would suggest because these borrowers self-selected for affordability at current rates. But loss severity risk is higher because HPA may not provide the same cushion if values correct.

Still thinking through the implications for the framework. Will share an updated note before the next deal.

Wile

emails.subject: RE: Retirement timeline - transition planning
emails.body:
R,

Thank you for the conversation last week. I want to address the model and knowledge transfer topic directly.

There are three things I am genuinely concerned about:

1. The VBA documentation is still incomplete. Anyone trying to modify the waterfall engine without understanding the subroutine dependencies could break the model silently. It would still produce outputs but they could be wrong.

2. The CDR framework has evolved over six years in ways that are not fully captured in the model risk document. The overlays, adjustments, and judgment calls I apply are in my head and in my emails more than in any formal document.

3. The rating agency relationships and my read of how Moody's and S&P apply their criteria to our specific deal structures is genuinely tacit. It took years to develop.

I am not trying to make myself indispensable. I want to make sure whoever takes this over can do it properly. Let's talk about what a real handover looks like.

Wile

emails.subject: Final deals and model notes
emails.body:
R,

As we finalize my last few months I want to put a few things in writing.

On the CDR framework: the most important thing for my successor to understand is that the numbers in the assumption tab are not arbitrary. Every basis point adjustment has a rationale. The geographic concentration overlays, the seasoning adjustments, the state-specific penalties - they are all documented in my emails going back to 2019. The model risk document captures the framework at a high level but the reasoning behind specific deals is in the deal communications.

On the Excel model: please do not let anyone modify the VBA without a proper documentation exercise first. I know T. Roadrunner has been asking about this. It is the right ask. The model works correctly as-is but it is fragile to uninformed changes.

I will do my best to finish the VBA documentation before I leave. But if I run out of time, the logic is in the code - it just needs someone patient enough to trace through it.

Wile

emails.subject: Handover notes - CDR framework summary
emails.body:
T, R,

As promised, a written summary of the CDR framework for the handover file.

Base CDR grid (LTV/FICO driven):
- WA LTV below 78%, WA FICO above 720: 1.8-2.2%
- WA LTV 78-85%, WA FICO 680-720: 2.5% floor
- WA LTV above 85% or FICO below 680: 3.5% floor

Adjustment overlays (applied additively):
- Geographic concentration (HHI above 0.15): +15 to +35bps depending on severity
- Single state above 25%: additional +10 to +20bps depending on state risk profile
- Seasoning below 6 months: +10 to +20bps. Above 18 months: -10 to -25bps credit
- State-specific overlays: FL judicial (+15bps when above 20% of pool), TX prolonged timeline (+15bps when above 25%)
- Temporary/event overlays: applied and documented at the time (COVID overlay is now retired)

This is the framework I have used since 2019 with minor refinements. It is not in the model itself - it lives in my head and in deal communications. I hope this write-up helps bridge that gap.

Wile

emails.subject: Last day notes
emails.body:
R,

A few final thoughts as I wrap up.

The model is in good shape mechanically. The waterfall engine works correctly for every deal structure we have used. The issue is not the model - it is the judgment layer on top of it. Anyone can run the spreadsheet. Knowing what numbers to put in it, and why, is the harder problem.

I have done my best to document the CDR framework. The VBA is still only partially documented - T. Roadrunner has my notes from our July session but the full code walkthrough was never finished. That is my biggest regret.

My recommendation: before the next deal, have someone sit down with the model and map every subroutine. It will take a week but it is worth it. And pull the deal communications for any specific assumption question - I tried to leave a paper trail.

It has been a privilege.

Wile
